 {"id":"phage_explorer-dx3","title":"Foundation: UI Design System & Visual Language","description":"## UI Design System & Visual Language

Establishes coherent visual language across all 31 features to maintain consistency.

### Color Semantics
- Green (#22c55e): Good, conserved, stable, similar
- Yellow (#eab308): Caution, moderate, neutral
- Red (#ef4444): Warning, divergent, unstable, different
- Blue (#3b82f6): Informational, cool metrics
- Purple (#a855f7): Special, unique, notable
- Gray (#6b7280): Inactive, background, reference

### Graph/Sparkline Standards
- Height: 3-5 character rows for overlays
- Width: Full terminal width
- Scale: Auto-normalize, show min/max in labels
- Style: Braille dots for high resolution

### Panel/Modal Standards
- Border: Single line box drawing
- Header: Bold, centered, with close hint
- Padding: 1 character on all sides
- Max width: 80 characters (or terminal width - 4)

This is the foundational design system that all features must follow.","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2025-12-06T23:35:34.98277-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-06T23:58:14.201697-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-06T23:58:14.201697-05:00"}
 {"id":"phage_explorer-dxv","title":"Advanced: Pan-Phage Graph Pangenome & Structural Variant Cards","description":"Build graph pangenome over bundled phages via Rust petgraph + minimap2 bindings. Compress bubbles into variant records (SNP/indel/structural). Per-bubble metadata in SQLite. Mini ASCII graph ribbon with bubbles; press enter for variant card showing diff stats, donor lineage bar, overlapped genes. Shows modular mosaicism and reassortment. Novelty: High.","notes":"## PAN-PHAGE GRAPH PANGENOME & STRUCTURAL VARIANT CARDS

**Full Implementation from Roadmap**

### Concept
Build a graph pangenome over all bundled phages to expose conserved cores, accessory modules, recombination breakpoints, and mosaicism. Per-variant 'cards' show local diff, donor lineage hints, and functional impact.

### How to Build

**Graph Construction:**
- Rust (petgraph + rayon) for rGFA-like graph
- Align genomes with minimap2 bindings or rust-bio aligners
- Compress bubbles into variant records (SNP/indel/structural)

**Annotation Linkage:**
- Map genes, AMGs, defense/anti-defense loci onto graph nodes
- Tag breakpoints with recombination evidence

**Variant Cards:**
- Per-bubble metadata in SQLite:
  - Size, gene overlap, GC shift, donor similarity
- Query via WASM-compiled graph summaries

### TUI Features
- Mini ASCII graph ribbon with bubbles highlighted
- Press enter to pop 'variant card':
  - Diff stats (size, gene count)
  - Donor lineage bar
  - Overlapped genes list
- Jump sequence grid to variant locus
- Filter by: size, function, donor
- Core vs accessory genome coloring

### Why It's Good
- Shows modular mosaicism and reassortment
- Surfaces hotspots for host range or defense evasion
- Reveals conserved backbone vs exchanged islands
- Goes beyond linear diffs to real evolutionary story

### Implementation Stack
- Rust+WASM graph builder (petgraph, rust-bio)
- minimap2 binding for alignments
- SQLite for variant cards
- TS/Ink UI for bubble strips and drilldowns

Novelty: 9/10, Pedagogical: 9/10, Wow Factor: 9/10","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2025-12-07T00:17:39.546693-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-23T14:25:31.797308075-06:00","closed_at":"2025-12-23T14:25:31.797308075-06:00","close_reason":"Out of scope - graph pangenome construction with minimap2/petgraph is infrastructure-heavy overkill","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"phage_explorer-dxv","depends_on_id":"phage_explorer-ojb","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-07T00:20:52.133844-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]}
-{"id":"phage_explorer-dy7l","title":"EPIC: Rust/WASM Acceleration Phase 2 (hot loops + zero-copy)","description":"# Overview
Phage Explorer already uses Rust/WASM in a few critical places (sql.js for SQLite, wasm-compute for some analytics, and WASM spatial-hash bond detection for 3D structures). This epic plans the **next wave** of Rust/WASM work to reduce latency, jank, and "waiting" across the web app and workers.

## Why this epic exists
We are now hitting the natural limits of "optimize the JS" approaches in a few hot paths:
- High-volume k-mer pipelines (counting, sketching, similarity) still pay JS overhead (string slicing, Map/Set churn).
- Some analyses (dotplots, diffs, PCA) can become noticeably slow on mid-range laptops and older mobile devices.
- Worker isolation prevents UI freezes, but it does **not** make the computation itself fast; it can still feel like "stuck at 20%".

Rust/WASM is our lever to:
- Make heavy loops tighter (fewer allocations, better cache locality)
- Use typed-array APIs that avoid JS object churn
- Enable optional zero-copy paths (SharedArrayBuffer) when COOP/COEP is available

## Global constraints / principles
- **Modern browsers stay fastest.** New WASM paths must not regress latest Chrome/Safari/Firefox.
- **Old browsers still work.** Every new WASM path must have a safe fallback (JS or a simpler kernel).
- **Keep WASM small & modular.** Avoid pulling huge crates into the always-loaded module. Prefer worker-local dynamic imports for optional features.
- **Design the boundary first.** WASM wins disappear if we bounce giant strings/JSON across the boundary every call.

## Success metrics (user-facing)
- Overlay open-to-first-render time is consistently low (no long "busy" hangs).
- Heavy overlays feel interactive quickly via progressive refinement (coarse-first).
- Minimal main-thread stalls and fewer large GC pauses.

## Implementation strategy (high level)
1. Build a reusable data plane: byte-based sequence transfer + stable WASM loader patterns.
2. Ship high-impact kernels first: k-mer counting/sketching, dotplot, diff.
3. Only then migrate secondary analytics (PCA and other worker primitives).

## Non-goals
- Rewriting the whole app into WASM.
- Large monolithic WASM bundles that slow down initial load.
- Removing existing JS implementations unless the WASM version is proven superior and we keep an emergency fallback.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2025-12-30T20:33:49.580172918-06:00","updated_at":"2025-12-30T20:33:49.580172918-06:00","labels":["perf","roadmap","wasm","web"]}
+{"id":"phage_explorer-dy7l","title":"EPIC: Rust/WASM Acceleration Phase 2 (hot loops + zero-copy)","description":"# Overview
Phage Explorer already uses Rust/WASM in a few critical places (sql.js for SQLite, wasm-compute for some analytics, and WASM spatial-hash bond detection for 3D structures). This epic plans the **next wave** of Rust/WASM work to reduce latency, jank, and "waiting" across the web app and workers.

## Why this epic exists
We are now hitting the natural limits of "optimize the JS" approaches in a few hot paths:
- High-volume k-mer pipelines (counting, sketching, similarity) still pay JS overhead (string slicing, Map/Set churn).
- Some analyses (dotplots, diffs, PCA) can become noticeably slow on mid-range laptops and older mobile devices.
- Worker isolation prevents UI freezes, but it does **not** make the computation itself fast; it can still feel like "stuck at 20%".

Rust/WASM is our lever to:
- Make heavy loops tighter (fewer allocations, better cache locality)
- Use typed-array APIs that avoid JS object churn
- Enable optional zero-copy paths (SharedArrayBuffer) when COOP/COEP is available

## Global constraints / principles
- **Modern browsers stay fastest.** New WASM paths must not regress latest Chrome/Safari/Firefox.
- **Old browsers still work.** Every new WASM path must have a safe fallback (JS or a simpler kernel).
- **Keep WASM small & modular.** Avoid pulling huge crates into the always-loaded module. Prefer worker-local dynamic imports for optional features.
- **Design the boundary first.** WASM wins disappear if we bounce giant strings/JSON across the boundary every call.

## Success metrics (user-facing)
- Overlay open-to-first-render time is consistently low (no long "busy" hangs).
- Heavy overlays feel interactive quickly via progressive refinement (coarse-first).
- Minimal main-thread stalls and fewer large GC pauses.

## Implementation strategy (high level)
1. Build a reusable data plane: byte-based sequence transfer + stable WASM loader patterns.
2. Ship high-impact kernels first: k-mer counting/sketching, dotplot, diff.
3. Only then migrate secondary analytics (PCA and other worker primitives).

## Non-goals
- Rewriting the whole app into WASM.
- Large monolithic WASM bundles that slow down initial load.
- Removing existing JS implementations unless the WASM version is proven superior and we keep an emergency fallback.","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2025-12-30T20:33:49.580172918-06:00","updated_at":"2025-12-31T07:05:41.022099277-06:00","labels":["perf","roadmap","wasm","web"]}
 {"id":"phage_explorer-dy7l.1","title":"Perf: dev-only instrumentation for worker compute stages (latency + cancellation)","description":"# Goal
Make performance work data-driven by instrumenting worker computations with:
- stage timings
- cancellation rate (stale requests aborted/ignored)
- end-to-end latency (request → first preview → final)

This is dev-only; we do **not** add telemetry.

# Background
A lot of perceived slowness is not just raw compute time:
- we compute too much before showing anything
- we fail to cancel stale requests when the user changes phage/overlay
- we do repeated work because caches aren’t visible

If we can see timings and cancellation, we can iterate intelligently.

# Scope
- Add lightweight timing hooks around worker entry points:
  - analysis.worker
  - dotplot.worker
  - comparison.worker
  - structure.worker

- Surface metrics in a dev overlay (or existing benchmark overlay):
  - median + p95 latency over N runs
  - stage breakdown

# Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Developers can see a timing breakdown for at least one heavy overlay.
- [ ] Cancellation of stale work is visible (so we can verify it’s working).
- [ ] Instrumentation is dev-only and does not ship noisy logs in prod.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-30T21:15:23.860649607-06:00","updated_at":"2025-12-30T22:14:44.0502011-06:00","closed_at":"2025-12-30T22:14:44.0502011-06:00","close_reason":"Created perf-instrumentation.ts module with: (1) startOperation()/finish() timing hooks, (2) Ring buffer for last 100 samples per operation type, (3) Aggregate stats (mean/median/p95/cancellation rate), (4) printReport() for console output, (5) window.__perfInstrumentation dev tools access. Integrated into ComputeOrchestrator runAnalysis* methods with getPerfStats() and printPerfReport() accessors.","labels":["devtools","perf","web","worker"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"phage_explorer-dy7l.1","depends_on_id":"phage_explorer-dy7l","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2025-12-30T21:15:23.861958144-06:00","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

 {"id":"phage_explorer-xivy","title":"Web Feature Parity & Mobile Excellence","description":"# Epic: Web Feature Parity & Mobile Excellence

## Executive Summary

This epic represents the strategic initiative to achieve full feature parity between the TUI and Web versions of Phage Explorer, while simultaneously establishing the Web version as a world-class mobile experience. The goal is not mere replication but intelligent adaptation—leveraging the unique strengths of each platform.

## Background & Rationale

### Current State Analysis

**TUI (Terminal UI) - 1100+ lines App.tsx**
- 50+ components, 40+ specialized analysis overlays
- 6 fully-implemented interactive simulations
- 56+ keyboard shortcuts across 7 categories
- Progressive experience system (novice→intermediate→power)
- Complete genomic analysis toolkit

**Web (Browser) - Current State**
- 43 overlay types defined, many incomplete
- 10 simulation types registered, visualizers partial
- 40+ keyboard shortcuts (good coverage)
- Mobile support exists but cell sizes problematic
- Canvas rendering works but needs optimization

### The Gap

1. **Mobile UX**: Cells too small for letters at default zoom; orientation changes not leveraged
2. **Analysis Overlays**: ~12 overlays incomplete or missing visualizations
3. **Simulation Visualizers**: 6 simulations need complete visualization components
4. **Progressive Disclosure**: Experience gating exists in state but not wired to UI
5. **3D Model**: Quality presets work but mobile-specific optimizations missing

## Strategic Goals

1. **100% Feature Parity**: Every TUI feature accessible via Web
2. **Mobile-First Excellence**: Touch-optimized, orientation-aware, readable at all sizes
3. **World-Class UX**: Discoverable, learnable, powerful
4. **Performance**: 60fps rendering, <100ms interactions, offline-capable
5. **Accessibility**: WCAG AA compliance, keyboard-only navigation, screen reader support

## Success Metrics

- All 40+ TUI overlays functional in Web
- All 6 simulations with complete visualizers
- Mobile NPS > 50 (Net Promoter Score)
- Lighthouse Performance > 90
- Time-to-Interactive < 3s on 3G

## Architecture Principles

1. **Shared Core**: Maximum code reuse via @phage-explorer/core
2. **Platform Adaptation**: TUI-optimized ≠ Web-optimized; adapt intelligently
3. **Progressive Enhancement**: Core features work without JS; enhanced with it
4. **Lazy Loading**: Load analysis algorithms on-demand via Web Workers
5. **Responsive First**: Design mobile, enhance for desktop

## Pillars of Work

### Pillar 1: Mobile Sequence Grid Excellence
Fix the current mobile grid issues and make it genuinely useful for genomic exploration.

### Pillar 2: Responsive & Orientation-Aware Layout  
Intelligent layout adaptation for portrait/landscape and all device sizes.

### Pillar 3: Complete Analysis Overlay Parity
Implement all 40+ TUI overlays with Web-optimized visualizations.

### Pillar 4: Simulation Visualizer Parity
Complete all 6 simulation visualizers with interactive controls.

### Pillar 5: Keyboard/Command Parity
Ensure all TUI hotkeys work in Web with proper conflict resolution.

### Pillar 6: Progressive Disclosure & UX Polish
Wire experience levels to UI, implement onboarding, feature tours.

### Pillar 7: 3D Visualization Parity
Mobile-optimized 3D with LOD, touch controls, quality presets.

### Pillar 8: Performance & Offline
Service worker optimization, lazy loading, cache strategies.

## Dependencies

- Existing @phage-explorer/core algorithms (complete)
- Existing @phage-explorer/state store (complete)  
- Web Workers infrastructure (exists, needs expansion)
- Canvas rendering system (exists, needs optimization)

## Timeline Estimate

Not providing time estimates per project guidelines. Work should be prioritized by impact and dependencies.

## Risk Factors

1. WebGL/WebGPU compatibility across mobile browsers
2. Memory constraints on older mobile devices
3. Touch gesture conflicts with browser gestures
4. IndexedDB storage limits for large genomes

 {"id":"phage_explorer-xj99","title":"INVESTIGATE: Mobile shows different bottom nav than ControlDeck","description":"## Problem

During Playwright testing of phage-explorer.org on mobile viewport (iPhone 14 Pro), the bottom navigation showed:
- SEARCH | DNA | GO TO | 3D | MORE

But the ControlDeck component I reviewed/modified has:
- Prev | DNA/AA | 3D | Menu | Next

This means either:
1. ControlDeck is not being rendered on mobile
2. There's a different mobile nav component being used
3. ControlDeck changes were not deployed
4. There's conditional rendering based on some state

## Evidence

Screenshots from Playwright testing show the SEARCH/DNA/GO TO/3D/MORE navigation on mobile. The Prev/Next phage navigation buttons (added to ControlDeck) are not visible.

## Impact

If ControlDeck with Prev/Next isn't rendering on mobile, users still cannot navigate between phages on touch devices - which was the original problem we were trying to fix.

## Investigation Needed

1. Find what component renders the mobile bottom nav
2. Determine why ControlDeck isn't being used
3. Either fix the rendering condition OR add Prev/Next to the correct component

## Files to Check

- packages/web/src/App.tsx (where ControlDeck is rendered)
- packages/web/src/components/mobile/*.tsx
